6. Bayesian Parameter Estimation

Let be the average photon rate arriving at sensor pixel. Let the data be the number of photons measured in some time interval. We can set it up as a hypothesis test where and . But what if we just want to predict the value of ? Parameter estimation emerges as limit of multi-hypothesis test.

In the Bayesian formulation, the parameter of interest is modeled as a random variable and the observations are realizations of a random variable .

Given a prior and a model , the posterior is

6.1. Bayes Risk

Let be the cost of estimating as . We choose our estimator to minimize the expected cost over and , i.e.

(6.2)

We choose to minimize the conditional risk pointwise for :

Examples of loss functions:

  • Minimum absolute error (MAE): . The optimal estimator is the median of the posterior:

    (6.4)

  • Minimum uniform cost (MUC): is if otherwise zero. In the limit as , the MUC estimate is the mode of the posterior, i.e. the MAP estimate. I.e. the MAP estimator is viewed as saying all errors are equally bad.

    (6.5)

  • Minimum mean squared error (MMSE) cost, also called Bayes least squares (BLS). The optimal estimator is the mean of the posterior belief

    (6.6)

    Proof

    □

  • Robust loss functions that look quadratic near zero and linear far away include the Huber loss, pseudo-Huber loss, and the log-cosh loss.

6.2. Performance of BLS Estimator

The error is a random variable defined as:

(6.7)

The bias is , and the bias-variance decomposition is expressed as

(6.8)

Remarks

  • In the Bayesian case, the bias and covariance are deterministic functions of the estimator function because we take an expectation over . Hence we can make any estimator unbiased by subtracting off the bias. In the nonbayseain case, we cannot do this.
  • Unlike frequentist estimation, the estimator and error do not necessarily have the same covariance.
Proposition 6.1. The BLS estimate is unbiased, i.e. . The error covariance matrix is the expected covariance of the posterior belief, i.e.
Proof
  • Trivial

□

Theorem 6.1 (BLS Orthogonality). An estimator is the BLS estimator if and only if the associated estimation error is orthogonal to any (vector-valued) function of the data, i.e.

(6.9)

Proof

(6.10)

To prove orthogonality implies BLS, set equal to since from above we have

□

Intuitively, we’d expect this out of an estimator than minimizes MSE there is no further processing to reduce the error covariance of the estimate since the error is just noise. Suppose there exists that is correlated with the data. Then consider adding a small correction for some matrix and scalar . DIfferentiating by at zero yields a nonzero slope that we can exploit. Contradiction with minimal risk.

Remark. In practice, we optimize the Bayes risk relative to a parameterized family of functions referred to as the hypothesis class, e.eg. linear architectures and neural networks.
